text: The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, carefully lifting the peritoneum to expose underlying structures.
The video shows a femoral hernia on the right side, accompanied by an obturator hernia.
The surgeon begins developing the peritoneal flap, carefully separating the peritoneal layer to expose underlying structures.
The medial ligament of the patient is observed to be not very prominent.
The surgeon begins the development of the peritoneal flap, positioning it approximately one to two centimeters above the arcuate line.
The arcuate line is visible well below the area where the surgeon initiates the peritoneal flap development.
The surgeon begins developing the peritoneal flap, moving towards the medial landmarks, with the pubis visible as a reference point.
The surgeon does not attempt to identify Cooper's ligament due to anticipated difficulty in this specific case.
The surgeon begins developing the peritoneal flap, starting at a point approximately one to two centimeters above the arcuate line.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, progressing towards the pubic region, with visible tissue manipulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the hernias and begins the reduction process, focusing on the femoral hernia. The hernias do not contain bowel, but there is visible peritoneal invagination into the space.
The surgeon isolates and identifies the femoral hernia, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks surrounding the femoral hernia: Cooper's ligament posteriorly, the lacunar ligament medially, and the iliopubic tract anteriorly.
The surgeon reduces the femoral hernia by gently manipulating and repositioning the herniated tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the vein on the lateral aspect of the surgical field, adjacent to the femoral hernia site.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the femoral hernia, avoiding aggressive dissection lateral to it to prevent potential major vascular injury.
The surgeon reduces the obturator hernia, carefully manipulating the herniated tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgical field reveals fat-containing tissue with visible peritoneal indigenation into the space, which could potentially act as a lead point for bowel incarceration or strangulation.
The surgeon begins developing the retropubic dissection, a step commonly performed in female patients.
During the inguinal hernia repair in women, the surgeon sacrifices the round ligament.
The surgeon completes the transection of the tissue, which was previously identified as having peritoneal indigenation.
The surgeon begins developing an adequate pocket in the preperitoneal space, preparing the area for mesh placement during the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement following the transection of the round ligament during the inguinal hernia repair procedure.
The iliac vessels are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes a vein, making it visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes a nerve, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon has developed a well-defined pocket in the surgical area, preparing it for the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the previously developed pocket, ensuring it covers the exposed iliac vessels, nerve, and vein.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh is positioned well below Cooper's ligament for adequate coverage.
The surgeon fixates the mesh at multiple points along Cooper's ligament to prevent migration, ensuring it remains positioned posteriorly for optimal integration and coverage of the defects.
The surgeon performs a straightforward flap closure of the peritoneum, with no visible holes present in the tissue.
